overview: As the renewed war entered its second week, Iran hardened from rhetoric into action: Tehran said it is suspending its commitments to the interim deal with Washington — dragging the dormant nuclear file back into the fight — while warning that 'no political border' will be safe if the strikes continue. It made the threat concrete by broadening its retaliation onto US allies: repeated Iranian strikes sent plumes of smoke over Kuwait's Mangaf district, Jordan shot down incoming missiles, and sirens sounded in Bahrain. The US, which says it has completed another round of strikes, is still hitting bridges and water plants across southern Iran; Iranian outlets now put the July death toll near 50. The one crack of daylight is economic — Iraq signed 48 deals in Washington, including a pipeline to bypass Hormuz entirely.

keyPoints.text: Iran says it is suspending its commitments to the interim nuclear deal with the US as the two exchange attacks, reviving the nuclear file it had shelved.
Iran broadened strikes onto US allies: repeated attacks hit Kuwait's Mangaf area, Jordan downed missiles and sirens sounded in Bahrain, warning 'no political border' is safe.
The US says it has completed the latest round of strikes; bridges and water plants across southern Iran hit for a seventh straight day, with Iranian outlets putting the July toll near 50.
The US keeps surging force — F-16s, F-35s and, per Axios, dozens of refueling tankers to Israel — as the reimposed port blockade tightens on Iran.
Iraq signed 48 deals with US firms in Washington, including rebuilding an Iraq-Syria crude pipeline that could route oil around the closed Strait of Hormuz.
With Hormuz shut and the US enforcing a counter-blockade, diesel has topped $5 a gallon and regular gasoline nears $4 — about $1.25 above a year ago.

watch.text: Whether Iran's suspension of its interim-deal commitments becomes a move on the nuclear file itself — expelling monitors further or resuming enrichment — now that IAEA access is already suspended.
Tehran's warning that 'no political border' is safe, tested against fresh strikes on Kuwait, Jordanian intercepts and Bahraini sirens as retaliation spreads across the Gulf.
Whether the incoming F-16s, F-35s and refueling tankers presage the expanded US operations Trump was reported weighing — deeper strikes rather than only infrastructure.

stories.title: Iran suspends its commitments to the interim deal
stories.summary: Tehran moved from words to a formal step, saying it is suspending its commitments to the interim deal with the United States as the two sides trade blows, according to PBS and Al Jazeera. Al Jazeera reported Iran suggested the June memorandum of understanding is now 'suspended' amid Iranian accounts that roughly 50 people have been killed in US strikes this month. The move follows a week in which an Iranian official declared negotiating during war 'over' and Tehran called the fight an 'existential war'; suspending the deal formalizes that rupture and reaches back to the nuclear file the June framework had tried to freeze. Cutting the other way, UN Secretary-General António Guterres — the world body's top official — has called for a new diplomatic push after strikes on civilian areas.
stories.explainer: The interim deal was the thread that kept the war's nuclear dimension dormant: a set of mutual commitments that, however frayed, gave both sides something to point to. Suspending it removes that guardrail just as IAEA inspectors are already locked out, meaning there is now neither a live agreement nor independent monitoring over Iran's most sensitive facilities. A combatant that pockets its nuclear commitments mid-war is signaling it may reopen enrichment or expel the last observers — the escalation that most alarms outside powers, because it converts a conventional war into a proliferation crisis.

stories.title: Iran widens its retaliation onto US allies
stories.summary: As the renewed conflict entered a second week, Iran launched a wave of attacks against the US-aligned states around it rather than against American soil. The Guardian reported Kuwait said civilian sites and infrastructure were targeted, Jordan shot down incoming missiles, and sirens sounded in Bahrain, home to the US Fifth Fleet. Al Jazeera footage showed towering plumes of smoke over Kuwait's Mangaf district after repeated Iranian strikes. The escalation came with a matching threat: Iran warned, per The Hill, that if US strikes persist 'no political border' will be safe — an explicit promise to hit the neighbors hosting American forces. A leaked US assessment reported earlier concluded Iran retained roughly 70% of its ballistic missiles after the spring campaign, evidence it can sustain this regional fight.
stories.explainer: Hitting the US through the Gulf states that shelter its bases raises the cost of the American campaign without crossing the line of a strike on US soil, while pressuring Kuwait, Jordan, Bahrain and their neighbors to lean on Washington for a ceasefire. It is also the exact mechanism by which a US-Iran duel becomes a regional war: a district ablaze in Kuwait or missiles over Amman can turn a hedging government into a full belligerent, which is why the 'no political border' framing is so dangerous — it treats every US partner as a legitimate target.

stories.title: Seventh day of strikes hits bridges and water plants
stories.summary: US strikes hit Iran for a seventh consecutive night, with the US military saying it had completed the latest round even as disruptions to Gulf shipping mounted. The campaign has pushed from military sites into civilian infrastructure — bridges, an airport and the power grid around the port of Bandar Abbas, and now water systems in the south that Iran says left roughly 10,000 people without supply. Iranian outlets, per Al Jazeera, now put the toll of the July strikes at around 50 killed. US Central Command has framed the operation as continuing to degrade Iran's military, but commentators across CNN, The New York Times and NPR — which asked bluntly 'why is it so hard for the US to win wars?' — warn Trump risks another open-ended 'forever war' with no resolution in sight.
stories.explainer: The shift from military targets to water, power and transport is the logic of strategic coercion — squeezing civilian life to force a government back to the table — but it rarely works quickly and deepens the humanitarian toll fastest. Cutting water to villages in mid-summer is both a survival crisis and a propaganda gift to Tehran, which frames each strike as a war crime; historically, infrastructure bombing hardens a population's resolve rather than breaking it, which is why 'strategic shift' and 'no closer to resolution' keep appearing in the same reports.

stories.title: US surges warplanes and tightens the blockade
stories.summary: The Pentagon is reinforcing the region as the war reignites. Air & Space Forces Magazine reported the US is deploying F-16 and F-35 fighter jets to the Middle East, and Axios reported Washington is sending dozens of aerial-refueling planes to Israel as Iranian escalation looms. Al Jazeera examined what to expect after the US reimposed its naval blockade on Iran's ports — pressure building on the Iranian public and on global energy markets. The buildup gives concrete shape to the Wall Street Journal's earlier report that Trump was leaning toward expanding US operations against Iran. Refueling tankers in particular are the enabler for sustained long-range air operations, so their forward deployment is read as preparation for a heavier, longer campaign rather than the current nightly strikes.
stories.explainer: Force posture often signals intent more reliably than statements. Fifth-generation F-35s add stealth penetration against Iran's surviving air defenses, while a fleet of tankers extends how far and how long any strike package can operate — the difference between harassing raids and a campaign able to hold targets deep inside Iran at will. Positioning them now, alongside the reimposed port blockade, suggests Washington is building the option for a wider war even if it has not yet decided to fight one.

stories.title: The war narrows around Hormuz as fuel prices climb
stories.summary: With the Strait of Hormuz closed since 12 July and US forces enforcing a counter-blockade — disabling tankers from the air and, this week, boarding the M/T Wen Yao — the conflict has increasingly centered on the waterway itself, described by NBC as a war now 'all about the Strait of Hormuz'. NPR reported that recognizing Iran's claim over the strait could set a dangerous precedent for other contested waterways. The energy shock is biting at home: US diesel has topped $5 a gallon and regular gasoline is nearing $4, according to AAA figures cited by the Guardian — roughly $1.25 higher than a year ago.
stories.explainer: About a fifth of the world's oil moves through Hormuz, a chokepoint so narrow that a few small islands sit astride its lanes, which is why closing it produced the largest supply disruption in the history of the oil market. The fear NPR flags is precedent: if force can convert a country's coastline into control of an international strait, other powers may be tempted to do the same at chokepoints like Malacca or Bab-el-Mandeb.

stories.title: Iraq courts Washington with a Hormuz workaround
stories.summary: Iraq signed 48 deals with US companies during a visit by its prime minister to Washington, Al Jazeera reported, in a rare piece of constructive economic news amid the war. Among them is an agreement to rebuild the long-defunct Iraq-Syria crude oil pipeline, a route that could carry oil to the Mediterranean and bypass the Strait of Hormuz entirely. For Baghdad — which shares a long border with Iran and hosts US forces Iran has threatened — the deals are a bet on staying aligned with Washington while insulating its own oil exports from the chokepoint at the center of the war.
stories.explainer: Every barrel that can leave the region without passing through Hormuz weakens Iran's central form of leverage. A working Iraq-Syria pipeline would give Gulf-adjacent crude an alternative artery to the Mediterranean, which is why such projects — dormant for years — suddenly attract investment the moment the strait is closed: the war has made bypassing Hormuz worth almost any price.

stories.title: Nuclear dimension lingers at Bushehr
stories.summary: Satellite imagery published last week showed fresh damage inside Bushehr, Iran's largest nuclear power plant, from strikes dated between 7 and 12 July — the window in which the ceasefire collapsed. With IAEA safeguards access suspended since strikes on Natanz earlier in the war, and Iran now suspending its interim-deal commitments, outside monitors are left reliant on satellites to gauge the condition of sensitive facilities. As US strikes push deeper into Iran's south — Bushehr sits on the Gulf coast near the newly targeted infrastructure — the risk of damage to a fueled reactor remains a background hazard to the whole campaign.
stories.explainer: Bushehr is a civilian power reactor, not an enrichment site, so the danger near it is radiological rather than proliferation: damage to a fueled reactor's cooling or containment could release contamination across the Gulf regardless of who is blamed. With inspectors locked out, satellite analysis is the only independent window into whether the war is edging toward a nuclear-safety crisis.

stories.title: Detainee off-ramp stays in dispute
stories.summary: The release of Dena Karari, a dual US-Iranian citizen detained since December 2024, which President Trump announced on 15 July as a possible opening, remains in dispute even as both sides step up attacks. With Iran now formally suspending its interim-deal commitments and declaring wartime negotiation 'over', the thin channel of indirect contacts that the release briefly seemed to open has little left to show. Separately, the family of Craig Foreman, a Briton jailed in Iran, said his sentence had been extended by two years for speaking to the media from prison.
stories.explainer: Detainee releases are normally the lowest-cost trust signal in US-Iran diplomacy, which is why a contested one matters: if the sides cannot even agree on whether a hand-over happened, the gesture that read as a path to climbdown loses its meaning — and Tehran's new refusal to talk under fire drains what remained of it.

stories.title: Lebanon presses to disarm Hezbollah
stories.summary: Lebanon's foreign minister, Youssef Raggi, said the government had decided to 'end Hezbollah's military presence', stressing that decisions on war and foreign policy are the 'exclusive prerogative of the Lebanese state'. The move to assert a state monopoly on force and disarm the Iran-backed group comes as Beirut tries to stay out of the widening US-Iran war, and after the 2026 Lebanon war displaced more than a sixth of the country's population.
stories.explainer: Hezbollah is the most powerful member of Iran's 'Axis of Resistance', the network of allied militias Tehran uses to project force. A Lebanese push to disarm it would strip Iran of its strongest regional lever just as Tehran most needs to threaten escalation — though forcing the issue risks reopening Lebanon's own internal conflict.

notes: Briefing synthesized from the day's wire feed (data/news.json, generated 2026-07-18T18:07Z by the scheduled GitHub Actions workflow). The manual RSS refresh via scripts/fetch-news.mjs was blocked by the environment's egress policy (403 on all news hosts), so this briefing draws on the workflow-committed feed rather than a fresh manual pull. This afternoon update tracks new developments since the morning briefing: Iran's suspension of its interim-deal commitments, its widening strikes onto US allies (Kuwait, Jordan, Bahrain) under a 'no political border' threat, the ~50 July death toll, and Iraq's Hormuz-bypass pipeline deals. Featured stories are ranked by systemic significance, scale, breadth, novelty and continuity; overview, keyPoints and stories describe the same events at different altitudes. Contested claims — casualty and water-outage figures, the '70%' missile-retention estimate, and Iranian-reported strikes — are attributed to their sources.
